metadata.title: Why Telling Claude the Topic Is the Wrong First Step
metadata.purpose: Slate cut of Candidate 02. Mechanism: Claude generates what you ask for — topic coverage — not what you need — outcome-aligned activities. Two prompts, 40 words apart, produce diverging lesson quality because only one names the outcome, prior knowledge, and misconception before the request.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = outcome-anchored prompt / aligned activities / student transfer; CRIMSON = topic-first prompt / coverage without transfer / the gap; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: NO Bloom's taxonomy deep-dive, NO full backward-design theory lecture, NO listing all eight fields of instructional brief, NO discussion of grading.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B13: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. Why Telling Claude the Topic Is the Wrong First Step.

props.segment: Why Telling Claude the Topic Is the Wrong First Step
props.command: Why Telling Claude the Topic Is the Wrong First Step

beats.narration_text: Musinique. Two teachers ask Claude for the same lesson. One gets a list of activities that cover the topic. The other gets a lesson that fixes the misconception her students actually carry.

card.copy: Why telling Claude the topic is the wrong first step
card.sub: the outcome has to come before the prompt

beats.narration_text: The tools this video covers were built for enterprises with engineering teams and compliance budgets. The musician in the van at 1 a.m. has access to the exact same machinery, for free, through a browser. The gap between knowing this exists and knowing how to use it is what this channel closes — one working piece of your promotion deck at a time.

artifactLines: The same tools the platforms use — pointed back at you.
Roll your own. Own the deck.

beats.narration_text: James teaches tenth-grade chemistry. He needs a lesson on photosynthesis. He types: Give me a lesson on photosynthesis. Monday, his students can say plants use sunlight to make food. Almost none can explain why they need to eat — which is load-bearing for the next unit.
beats.scene_description: A teacher in a chemistry classroom at a laptop, typing a prompt. The screen is visible showing a short, simple query. Students visible in the background at desks. Editorial documentary style.
beats.image_prompt: B02, high school chemistry classroom, a teacher seated at a laptop typing a short text query visible on screen, students at desks in the background, warm classroom lighting, editorial documentary desaturated style, flat newsprint tone, no brand logos
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] teacher at laptop typing a short prompt

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL] teacher at laptop typing a short prompt

beats.narration_text: Two prompts. Same topic. Same lesson request. The outputs diverge completely. They differ by forty words. Why?

card.copy: Same topic, same lesson request. The outputs diverge completely. They differ by 40 words. Why?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] THE QUESTION on screen

beats.narration_text: Here is how most teachers think about lesson-planning prompts. Describe the topic well. Claude knows photosynthesis. It will cover the important parts. The lesson will address what students need.

production_viz.label: naive assumption: topic -> Claude -> lesson -> student learning
production_viz.mechanic: Four TEAL blocks in a left-to-right chain: 'TOPIC', 'CLAUDE', 'LESSON', 'LEARNING'. Each slides in with an arrow. The chain looks solid and complete.
production_viz.note: the naive model — will be broken at 'LEARNING'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] naive chain: topic -> Claude -> lesson -> learning

beats.narration_text: But Claude does not know your learning target. It knows what you asked for. Topic coverage is not the same as learning design. And the gap between them is invisible in the output.

production_viz.label: the gap between coverage and learning
production_viz.mechanic: The B04 chain reappears. The arrow between 'LESSON' and 'LEARNING' turns CRIMSON and a gap opens between the two blocks. A serif label drops in over the gap: 'coverage does not equal learning'.
production_viz.note: the mechanism is the gap, not the lesson

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] gap between LESSON and LEARNING opens in crimson

beats.narration_text: Claude is an activity generator. Left to itself, it gives you activities. It does not know where your class is in the sequence, what students believe incorrectly, or what would count as evidence they learned this.

card.copy: Claude generates what you ask for — not what students need

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] section — Claude generates what you ask for

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] section — Claude generates what you ask for

beats.narration_text: Prompt A: Give me a lesson on fractions. Prompt B: Sixth-grade lesson on dividing fractions. Students believe dividing always makes things smaller — need one activity that forces this prediction into conflict, one that resolves it with a visual model.

production_viz.label: two prompts side by side
production_viz.mechanic: Two prompt window cards slide in side by side. Left (CRIMSON border): one short line 'Give me a lesson on fractions'. Right (TEAL border): four lines naming grade level, misconception, and activity types. A serif count appears under each: '6 words' vs '38 words'.
production_viz.note: the comparison — not length that matters, but what the words contain

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two prompt cards — short vs. outcome-anchored

beats.narration_text: Prompt A produces three definitional exercises. Prompt B produces a prediction exercise that forces students to encounter the conflict — six divided by a half equals twelve — before the lesson resolves it.

production_viz.label: diverging outputs under the two prompts
production_viz.mechanic: Under each prompt window from B07, three output activity chips drop in: Left (CRIMSON chips): 'Define fraction', 'Write the rule', 'Practice problems'. Right (TEAL chips): 'Predict: is 6 div 1/2 more or less than 6?', 'Visual model: fraction strips', 'Resolve the conflict'. The contrast is clear.
production_viz.note: the example from the card's example seed

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three CRIMSON activities vs. three TEAL aligned activities

beats.narration_text: Every piece of information in the outcome-anchored prompt — the grade level, the misconception, the activity type, the evidence — James already knew. He just had not written it down before opening Claude.

card.copy: He knew it all. He just hadn't written it down first.
card.attribution: — the only thing the second prompt adds

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] quote — he already knew it all

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] quote — he already knew it all

beats.narration_text: What changes when Claude knows the target? The warm-up surfaces the misconception he named. The activities build toward the connection he specified. The formative check matches his evidence definition. Alignment is not something Claude does. It is something the prompt enables.

production_viz.label: outcome-anchored chain: outcome -> aligned prompt -> aligned lesson -> transfer
production_viz.mechanic: Four TEAL blocks in a chain: 'NAMED OUTCOME', 'ANCHORED PROMPT', 'ALIGNED LESSON', 'STUDENT TRANSFER'. All connections are TEAL and solid. A serif label below the chain: 'alignment is what the prompt enables'.
production_viz.note: the fixed chain — all four blocks solid teal

beats.narration_text: The practice: before you open Claude, write one sentence — what will students be able to DO by the end? Not a topic. A performance. Students will explain why six divided by one-half equals twelve. That sentence is the prompt anchor. Every activity Claude generates either builds toward it or it doesn't.

production_viz.label: the one-sentence outcome — the practice
production_viz.mechanic: A blank notepad card appears. A handwriting-style text writes in: 'Students will explain why 6 div 1/2 = 12.' A TEAL bracket appears beside the sentence labeled 'outcome'. Below, a serif label: 'write this before you open Claude'.
production_viz.note: concrete and checkable — the one move

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the one-sentence outcome written on a notepad

beats.narration_text: Claude cannot write your learning outcome for you. You know the class. You know the misconception. You know what the next unit needs. The outcome has to come before the prompt — every time.

production_viz.label: two paths: topic first vs. outcome first
production_viz.mechanic: Two short path diagrams stacked. Top (CRIMSON): 'TOPIC -> PROMPT -> COVERAGE'. Bottom (TEAL): 'OUTCOME -> PROMPT -> TRANSFER'. A serif label beside each: 'without the outcome' / 'with the outcome'.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two paths — topic first vs. outcome first

beats.narration_text: Topic coverage is not learning design. Outcome before topic — every time. From Musinique.

card.copy: Topic coverage is not learning design.
card.sub: Name the outcome first. AI IN EDUCATION

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — topic is not learning design

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — topic is not learning design
